---
title: k-armed Bandit Problem
description: 'The exploration–exploitation tradeoff: ε-greedy, UCB, and gradient-bandit
  action selection.'
categories:
- Foundations
---

![k-armed bandit: estimated action values over time](/images/notes/k-armed-bandit-1.png)

Imagine a row of $k$ slot machines, each giving rewards according to its own unknown probability distribution. Your job is to decide which machine to pull, and how often, to get the highest total reward over time.

Each arm $a \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ has an unknown expected reward. When you choose an arm you get a reward, learn a little more about that arm, and give up the chance to try another — so the core challenge is the **exploration–exploitation tradeoff**.

At each time step $t$:

1. the agent selects an action $A_{t}$,
2. it receives a reward $R_{t}$,
3. it updates its estimate of that arm's value.

The estimated value of action $a$ is written $Q(a)$.

## Value-based methods

Estimate $q^{*}(a)$, the true value of action $a$, and use those estimates to decide.

**ε-greedy** — select the maximising action (exploitation) most of the time, but a random action with probability $\epsilon$ (exploration):

$$
\pi_t(a) =
\begin{cases}
1 - \epsilon & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_t(a') \\
\epsilon & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)** — add an uncertainty term to the highest estimated value:

$$
A_t = \arg\max_{a} \left[ Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]
$$

where $Q_{t}(a)$ is the current value estimate, $N_{t}(a)$ is the number of times $a$ has been selected before time $t$, $t$ is the total number of steps, and $c > 0$ controls the degree of exploration.

Value-based methods follow the error-correction principle — the update moves the current estimate toward the received reward by a fraction of the error:

$$
Q_{t+1}(A_t) = Q_t(A_t) + \alpha\,[R_t - Q_t(A_t)]
$$

## Gradient-based methods

Learn a numerical preference $H_{t}(a)$ for each action — the larger the preference, the more often the action is taken, though the preference has no interpretation in terms of reward. A softmax converts preferences into probabilities:

$$
\pi_t(a) = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b=1}^{k} e^{H_t(b)}}
$$

The update is stochastic gradient ascent: the preference for the action just taken, $A_{t}$, is adjusted by how the reward $R_{t}$ compares to a baseline $\bar{R}_{t}$ (usually the average of all rewards so far):

$$
\begin{align*}
H_{t+1}(A_t) &= H_t(A_t) + \alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)(1 - \pi_t(A_t)) \\
H_{t+1}(a) &= H_t(a) - \alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)\,\pi_t(a) \quad \forall\, a \neq A_t
\end{align*}
$$

The term $R_{t} - \bar{R}_{t}$ serves as the reinforcement signal.
